text:
The increasing severity of geological hazards and the urgent need for timely disaster relief operations in sudden geological emergencies make the application of remote sensing technology for geological hazard investigation and assessment extremely necessary. This technology can be integrated into the entire process of geological hazard investigation and evaluation, monitoring and early warning, disaster assessment, and mitigation, reflecting an inevitable trend in contemporary high-tech development. While remote sensing technology has been successfully employed in regional geological hazard investigations and evaluations, most experiences have remained at the level of extracting disaster points and conducting macro-regional evaluations. Research utilizing high-precision satellite remote sensing technology for fine interpretation and assessment of geological hazard points is relatively scarce, with most analyses of single-point geological hazards relying on aerial photographs. Particularly in the area of geological hazard monitoring, successful case studies are few. This paper uses examples from the Xuanhan landslide, the debris flow near the Luding hydropower station, bank collapse in the Three Gorges area, and the proposed Lijiang-Xianggelila Railway to present the following research findings from systematic studies: 

(1) A preliminary systematic methodology for studying colluvial landslides using high-precision satellite remote sensing technology has been established. This involves revising and enhancing the identification markers and interpretation methods for geological hazard remote sensing based on the causes and behavioral characteristics of colluvial landslides. The advantages and limitations of remote sensing technology in geological hazard studies are clarified, with specific instances demonstrating its role in hazard investigation, assessment, emergency response, and engineering design for hazard mitigation.

(2) Key remote sensing technologies in the study of colluvial landslides are discussed in detail, covering data source selection, image spectral correction, geometric correction, orthorectification, 3D remote sensing model establishment, and interferometric radar techniques. Targeted remote sensing image processing methodologies are proposed for different geological hazard research subjects, stages, and levels.

(3) Current popular image fusion techniques are discussed in conjunction with specific images. Considering that geological hazard research focuses on extracting signs of deformation and damage, a resolution fusion method based on area ratio transformation is recommended for remote sensing interpretation to enhance texture features. For image construction, resolution fusion methods based on Gram-Schmidt transformation or principal component transformation are suggested to maximize the retention of spectral characteristics in multispectral images, along with a new color transformation method for SPOT images.

(4) Based on extensive high-precision satellite image interpretation of colluvial landslide geological hazards and existing results, the plane morphology, internal structure, and behavioral characteristics of geological hazards have been thoroughly studied. This has supplemented and refined the interpretative markers and methods for remote sensing imagery, proposing a new method for estimating landslide volume. Using specific examples from the Baiyian Landslide and the debris flow near the Luding hydropower station, a methodology for extracting detailed features of geological hazards and conducting quantitative assessments using QuickBird images has been established.

(5) Integrating the engineering geology remote sensing interpretation project for the proposed railway line from Lijiang to Shangri-La, high-resolution SPOT images fused to a resolution of 5 meters combined with aerial photos have been utilized to extract the disaster-prone conditions and current development status of colluvial landslides in the study area. An assessment of the geological hazard distribution characteristics along the proposed line was conducted. Leveraging the advantages of remote sensing technology in extracting geological hazard risk assessment indices, a reliable evaluation index was established using a simple mathematical model: GM = (multiply from i=1 to n pi)^(1/n), closely aligning with the engineering geological conditions and formation mechanisms of developing colluvial landslides, providing a regional risk assessment of colluvial geological hazards in the study area. Comparative analysis indicates that this evaluation model is reliable, and the analysis results align well with the current state of geological hazard development, demonstrating practical value.

(6) A comparative analysis of pre-landslide, post-landslide, and post-remediation characteristics of the Xuanhan landslide was conducted using multi-temporal, high-resolution IKONOS and QuickBird satellite images. This analysis encompassed the formation conditions, development characteristics, signs of deformation and damage, and major disaster conditions, leading to the establishment of a high-precision 3D remote sensing model for the landslide area, providing a basis for landslide remediation engineering design.

(7) For the first time, multi-temporal QuickBird satellite images were utilized for monitoring bank collapses. A remote sensing technical methodology for collapse monitoring was established, enhancing the remote sensing interpretation work in the Three Gorges Reservoir area. Comparative studies of QuickBird satellite remote sensing images for two periods in specific sections of the Three Gorges Reservoir were conducted, delineating the distribution range of bank collapses associated with a reservoir water level of 139m during more than a year of water accumulation. The feasibility and accuracy of using high-precision satellite remote sensing technology to monitor bank collapse extent were analyzed, providing a basis for predicting bank collapse ranges at water levels of 156m and 175m in the Three Gorges Reservoir.

During the water storage process and operation of the reservoir, the original natural equilibrium conditions of the bank slopes are disrupted, causing changes in the shape and stability of the slopes, leading to geological hazards such as bank collapses, landslides, and rockfalls. After the formation of the reservoir, the geological environmental conditions of the surrounding areas will change significantly. Additionally, the rise in water levels causes local river erosion baselines and groundwater levels to increase, resulting in dynamic changes in water levels that intensely modify the reservoir's bank slopes. Instability of reservoir banks is a common mode of slope deformation in hydraulic and hydropower engineering. The Wudongde Hydropower Station, the first of four hydropower tiers on the lower Jinsha River, has an installed capacity of 8.7 million kW. Based on the normative water storage level of 975m and a dam height of 265m, the reservoir tail is located in the vicinity of Miji Village east of Panzhihua City, with a main reservoir length of 206.7 km. 

This paper categorizes the bank slope structural types in the reservoir area based on detailed field investigations of the geological environmental conditions, analyzing slope deformation and damage phenomena (landslides, rockfalls, and distribution patterns of hazardous rock development), studying the deformation damage modes and their triggering factors, and evaluating the stability of landslides, hazardous rocks, and bank slopes. Finally, a segmented stability evaluation of the banks is conducted, along with proposed countermeasures for bank instability prevention and control. The main conclusions of the paper are as follows: (1) The total length of the main river banks on both sides of the study area is 195.4 km, of which: soil bank slopes are 49.3 km, accounting for 25.23% of the total main river bank length; shallow metamorphic rock slopes are 41.3 km, accounting for 21.14%; carbonate rock slopes are 14.7 km, accounting for 7.52%; and clastic rock slopes are 90.1 km, accounting for 46.11%. The total length of the three main tributary banks is 27.5 km, all consisting of clastic rock slopes. (2) The structural types of the bank slopes in the reservoir area can be segmented into: 25 segments of soil bank slopes (4 of landslide-deposited soil bank slopes, 11 of collapse-accumulated soil bank slopes, and 10 of alluvial soil bank slopes); 12 segments of shallow metamorphic rock slopes (9 of forward-dipping shallow metamorphic rock slopes and 3 of backward-dipping shallow metamorphic rock slopes); 5 segments of carbonate rock slopes (3 of forward-gentle-dipping carbonate rock slopes, 1 of backward-gentle-dipping carbonate rock slopes, and 1 of backward-dipping carbonate rock slopes); and 20 segments of clastic rock slopes (5 of forward-gentle-dipping clastic rock slopes, 14 of backward-gentle-dipping clastic rock slopes, and 1 of oblique-gentle-dipping clastic rock slope). (3) The deformation damage phenomena of the bank slopes in the reservoir area are primarily characterized by landslides, rockfalls, and hazardous rock bodies, including 32 landslides (37.5% are super-large landslides; 40.6% are large landslides; and 21.9% are medium landslides), and 18 rockfalls and hazardous rock bodies (all are medium scale or above, with 89% being large and super-large). (4) The reformation types of damaged soil bank slopes are mainly erosion-abrasion type, retrogressive collapse type, and overall sliding type. (5) The deformation damage modes of rocky bank slopes can be categorized into four main types: gently-dipping layered rock bank slope sliding-tension-shear "three-stage" mode; forward-dipping layered rock bank slope sliding or planar sliding mode of structured layers; "sliding (bending)-shear" mode or "sliding-shear" mode along weak interlayer inclusions; backward-dipping layered rock bank slope "bending-toppling" mode; and oblique-layered rock slopes that remain relatively stable, showing deformation damage mainly as localized or small-scale rockfalls and landslides due to shallow fissures. (6) Rainfall, river erosion effects, and human engineering activities are the three main triggering factors for the deformation damage of bank slopes in the reservoir area. (7) Among the 32 landslides in the reservoir area, 19 are basically stable under current conditions, 6 have poor stability, and 7 are unstable; after water storage, 14 remain stable, 7 have poor stability, and 11 are unstable. Of the 18 rockfalls and hazardous rock bodies, 10 are generally stable under current conditions, 7 have poor stability, and 1 is unstable, with 9 rockfalls remaining stable and 5 showing poor stability; after water storage, hazardous rock bodies are situated above the water level, with stability not significantly affected, while the stability of some portions of the collapsed bodies below water level decreases. (8) In terms of quantitative stability evaluation of bank slopes, segments with good stability (stable, basically stable) account for 54.8% of the total number of segments and 83.3% of the total length, while segments with poor and very poor stability comprise a significant number, representing 45.28% of the total segments but only 26.7% of the total length. (9) Based on the actual conditions of the Wudongde reservoir area, following the three principles of "integrated prevention and control, rapid treatment of major issues, phased management, and localized comprehensive treatment," the main countermeasures for preventing and controlling bank instability are: monitoring and forecasting, preventing excessive infiltration of rainfall and surface water, preventing severe loading at the rear edge of slopes, mitigating river erosion effects, and implementing comprehensive management while preserving land resources.

Remote sensing technology is a comprehensive technique that processes and images electromagnetic wave information collected by sensor instruments from distant targets based on electromagnetic wave theory, allowing for the detection and identification of various ground features. Remote sensing geology is the discipline and technique that studies geological, mineral, disaster, and other related information and patterns based on the electromagnetic wave information of the radiation and reflection of ground objects collected by sensors. By integrating remote sensing and GIS technology, various objectives can be achieved, including topographic mapping, geological structural analysis, and preliminary surveys of surface and shallow mineralization areas. With the advancement of artificial satellite technology, obtaining satellite imagery has become increasingly easier, and utilizing remote sensing technology for preliminary geological surveys, topographical studies, and geological hazard assessments has become more cost-effective and faster. The middle and lower reaches of the Heilongjiang River have undergone severe land loss due to long-term erosion and scouring by river waters, resulting in significant economic losses, which adversely affects the stability and long-term development of political relations between the two countries. Therefore, studying the geological hazards of riverbank collapses along the Heilongjiang River, investigating the changes in riverbank conditions, researching the causes and factors leading to these geological hazards, and proposing plans and measures for prevention and control of such disasters are of great significance. This study utilizes multi-temporal remote sensing image data and topographic maps to create Digital Raster Graphics (DRG) and remote sensing orthophotos. Based on this, it interprets information regarding the riverbank, bank protection structures, riverbank collapse geological hazards, and fault structures in the Heihe–Fuyuan section of the Heilongjiang River, classifying the geological landforms, lithological bodies, valleys, and bank protection works in this river segment, and statistically analyzing, comparing, and assessing the changes in riverbank conditions and riverbank collapses. The total area of erosion in the Heihe–Fuyuan section of the Heilongjiang River is 86.583 km², with 41.474 km² of erosion along the riverbank and 45.109 km² of erosion along islands and shoals; the total area of deposition is 115.364 km², with 30.637 km² of deposition along the riverbank and 84.726 km² along islands and shoals. Overall, deposition is greater than erosion, but erosion along the riverbank is more severe than deposition. The shape characteristics of the bank are key factors influencing the occurrence of riverbank collapse geological hazards and the changes in riverbanks. Based on their shape characteristics, banks can be classified into compound concave banks, compound convex banks, and straight banks. Compound concave banks exhibit higher levels of occurrences and areas of riverbank collapse geological hazards, as well as the frequency and extent of changes in riverbanks, compared to convex banks, which generally experience deposition. Straight banks are less favorable concerning changes in riverbanks, collapses, and deposition. This is primarily because concave banks are severely eroded by the lateral impact of flow; on the other hand, sediment eroded from concave banks accumulates on convex banks due to the flow direction of the deep channel being directed away from the bank. Meanwhile, straight banks are relatively straight, with the river flow generally parallel to the bank, leading to poorer erosion and deposition and consequently a smaller rate of riverbank change. The type of lithological body of the banks significantly influences the occurrence of riverbank collapses and changes in riverbanks. Rock-based bank structures have strong hardness and erosion resistance, maintaining their integrity during river impacts, while soil-based banks are softer and more susceptible to erosion. In the same concave scenarios, the segments of soil-based banks exhibit significantly greater changes in riverbanks and riverbank collapse geological hazards than rock-based bank segments. Erosion and collapse conditions downstream are clearly more severe than upstream. The concentration of tributary inflows increases the overall flow rate and energy downstream, enhancing the capacity to erode banks, resulting in more significant changes in riverbanks and geological hazards. Moreover, geological hazards from erosion and collapse in mountainous river valleys are less severe compared to those in plain river valleys. Mountainous riverbanks typically consist of hard rock with strong erosion resistance, and the geometry of these valleys is usually straight with minimal curvature, leading to less severe erosion and collapse geological hazards in mountain river segment. Riparian vegetation has a certain impact on the occurrence of riverbank collapse geological hazards and changes in riverbanks. Studies indicate that, under similar lithological conditions and river channel situations, banks with well-developed vegetation such as native grasses and forests exhibit stronger erosion resistance, while those along agricultural lands and over-exploited forests suffer from significant erosion.

The prevention and mitigation of geological hazards on reservoir banks are critical scientific issues concerning the protection of the ecological environment, particularly the geological environment of reservoir areas. Based on a review and analysis of domestic and international research literature, coupled with years of practical research in the Three Gorges Reservoir area, the current state of research on reservoir bank geological hazards can be categorized into three aspects: changes in bank stability, mechanisms of failure, and methods for predictive assessment of bank reconstruction. Each of these areas has been systematically reviewed. The findings indicate that scholars both domestically and internationally place high importance on investigating the impact mechanisms of declining water levels on bank stability and deformation characteristics. Several new methods for predicting bank reconstruction have been proposed based on the Katchijin method. However, there is limited research on the effects of multi-cyclic variations in reservoir water levels on bank processes, the periodic soaking of water levels deteriorating the physical and mechanical properties of soil and rock, and research on material-like soil banks. The study highlights three critical directions for further research on geological hazards of reservoir banks that require significant attention: the geological environmental effects of reservoir banks, deformation and failure of material-like soil banks, and new technologies and methodologies for the management of geological hazards on reservoir banks.

The Three Gorges Hydropower Project on the Yangtze River is an unparalleled monumental engineering feat. The majority of the one million relocated residents have been resettled locally. The geological conditions along the reservoir are quite complex, with numerous landslides and collapses, significantly impacting the safety of the relocated populations. The government places great emphasis on the prevention and control of geological hazards in the resettlement areas along the Three Gorges Reservoir. Since the 1950s, investigations into the stability of the reservoir banks and geological hazards have been conducted, with explorations undertaken on several significant landslides, including effective engineering treatments for those with considerable impacts such as the Jibazi landslide, the Chain Cliff unstable rock mass, the Huanglashi landslide, and the Douyapeng landslide. Recently, billions of dollars have been allocated for large-scale geological hazard prevention and control in the current phase. According to a comprehensive survey conducted before this preventative effort, there are a total of 2,490 landslide and collapse disaster sites in the entire reservoir area. Among them, 581 sites related to the safety of a water storage level of 135 meters and the new resettlement sites of the second phase have been included in the current prevention plan. Different preventative measures have been implemented based on the risk level of landslides and collapses, the difficulty of prevention, and the mobility of affected objects. There have been engineering treatments for 198 sites, relocations of 232 sites, and enhanced monitoring and early warning systems for 151 sites. The prevention and control work has substantial social and economic benefits and presents a high degree of scientific and technical difficulty, and is currently in vigorous progress. The article discusses the characteristics of geological hazards in the reservoir area and the historical research on prevention, as well as the key points of the current research efforts and the main topics for future preventive studies.

The current slope rock types at the Xijiao Reservoir are primarily loamy soil and loess-like loamy soil, with collapse mechanisms largely attributed to lower erosion and upper settling failure. The current slopes can be categorized into the following types: 

2.1 Protected Slopes  
The protected slopes are mainly located below Quanan Tan Village on the left bank. There are generally two protection methods: the first involves a combination of slope revetment and slope cutting, primarily distributed along the left bank from station 0+000 to 0+500m, exhibiting current stability and good protective effects; the second is simple slope cutting, such as in the section from 1+000 to 3+300m, where the upper slope is stable but the lower section has been eroded due to fluctuating water levels, leading to potential long-term collapse in the upper sections as well.

2.2 Erosion and Collapse Type Slopes  
Erosion and collapse type slopes are one of the main slope types present in the reservoir area. The slopes have a near-vertical gradient, with visible vertical fractures and water discharge holes developed within 2 meters of the reservoir edge. The unloading zone typically varies from 3 to 6 meters in width, reaching up to more than ten meters at maximum. The width of unloading cracks generally ranges from 0.1 to 0.3 meters, with visible depths of about 2 to 3 meters. Following erosion of the lower part, the upper section collapses vertically in large blocks. Representative sections include the left bank from 4+700 to 5+600m, the right bank from 0+000 to 4+500m, and from 10+580 to 14+000m, characterized by specific slope and damage morphology.

2.3 Reservoir Water Modification Type Slopes  
Reservoir water modification type slopes are another main slope type existing in the reservoir area. The lower slope has been modified by long-term erosion due to the reservoir water level, resulting in a gentler gradient of approximately 20° to 30°, while the upper slope formed under the influence of the lower slope's modified terrain presents a gradient of 40° to 50°. Representative sections include the left bank from 5+600 to 9+870m and the right bank from 4+500 to 10+580m, characterized by specific bank slope and damage morphology.

5 Comprehensive Measures for Landslide Control Based on the aforementioned analysis, and taking into account various factors such as the severity of the current landslide, the importance of impacted objects, and the types of landslides, geological recommendations for the treatment of different sections and types of landslides are presented as follows. 

5.1 Management Measures The frequent fluctuation of reservoir water levels and wave-induced erosion of the slopes are the primary causes of landslides. Investigations indicate that the daily maximum water level variation in the Xijiao Reservoir can exceed 1 meter. From the perspective of reservoir operation management, it is essential to enhance dynamic monitoring, improve operational management strategies, and minimize rapid increases and decreases in water levels to mitigate the erosive effects of reservoir water on the slopes. Additionally, strengthen the management of surrounding irrigation and other water uses, ensuring proper drainage to reduce erosion and the saturation effects on the slope's soil and rock mass.

5.2 Engineering Measures The main measures for slope stabilization include rock dumping, gabion baskets, and soil backfill projects. Different measures should be employed based on varying destruction modes, damage severity, and the significance of affected structures along the banks. Since wave-induced erosion is the main cause of landslides, protective measures should be reinforced across all segments, particularly in areas of significant water level fluctuation (416-420m). For sections requiring urgent intervention, it is suggested to implement foundational rock dumping, retrofitting of gabion baskets at the lower slope, enhanced protection in water fluctuation zones, and soil backfill at higher elevations as comprehensive rehabilitation strategies. For reservoir tail and sections where action can be deferred, enhancement of protection in fluctuation zones combined with upper slope reduction methods should be employed.

(1) Our country is one of the nations with a high incidence of landslide geological hazards. The exacerbation of global climate change and human activity disturbances has led to a more severe degree of landslide damage in recent years, necessitating a more standardized, systematic, institutionalized, and timely approach to landslide geological hazard monitoring and research. Remote sensing will provide the most effective technical means for this purpose. (2) Utilizing remote sensing technology for landslide geological hazard monitoring can be conducted from four aspects: identification of landslide bodies, monitoring of landslide bodies, monitoring of influencing factors of landslides, and identification of landslide-prone areas with hazard analysis. (3) In the process of conducting remote sensing monitoring research on landslide geological hazards, attention must be paid to the application of high-precision DEM data and the selection of data sources. The exploration of high-precision DEM data application potential can enhance the precision and accuracy of landslide target identification; meanwhile, given the multitude of remote sensing data sources available, it is essential to scientifically select data sources according to the monitoring research needs of landslide hazards at different damage levels, striving to maximize the "cost-benefit ratio."

Landslides are one of the more severe geological hazard types in the reservoir area, with a total of 9 collapses recorded, resulting in an average linear density of 0.09 occurrences/km, accounting for 11.11% of total hazards. The most significant landslide in the reservoir area is located in the limestone exposure zone southwest of Daluzui (right bank of the Jinsha River); followed by the reverse slope area in the alpine canyon zone; and additionally, there are localized landslides in the alluvial terraced sections of the Jinsha River valley. The occurrence of landslides in the reservoir area is closely related to the terrain and landform, with the upstream section characterized by a middle to high mountain canyon, where localized slopes exceed 50°, creating conditions for the development of collapses and the potential for movement. Furthermore, differential weathering of weak rocks results in the formation of local mudstone troughs, isolating overlying sandstone, resulting in hazardous rocky bodies that are prone to failure. Moreover, the reservoir area experiences intense tectonic activity, leading to well-developed joint fractures in the rock mass, often resulting in several sets of structural surfaces. Landslides in the reservoir area primarily occur in the alluvial terraced sections of the Jinsha River valley to the west of the JInjiang Bridge, with past gold mining activities damaging the riverbanks, resulting in a decline in stability in localized sections, causing collapses. The alluvial terraces of the Jinsha River valley mainly consist of Quaternary loose alluvial and flood sediment deposits. Due to intense lateral erosion by the Jinsha River, coupled with significant downcutting, the toe of the Quaternary alluvial and flood deposits forming the riverbank is eroded, leading to an imbalance in the material above the slope, which, under the influence of gravity, causes the failure of the riverbank.

The initial project of the Danjiangkou Water Control Project, after 30 years of operation, has achieved a relatively stable condition in the soil bank slopes below the normal water level of 157 m. The remediation of the soft rock slopes of the red bed has been partially completed, with only minor impacts on the igneous rock, limestone, and slate slopes, which have not experienced significant bank collapses. Analysis of relevant data indicates that notable bank collapse phenomena are concentrated in the following slope sections: (1) The soil bank slopes between Pengjiagang and Luanshupa in Liuping Town, Yunxian County, exhibit a slope angle greater than 30° and heights ranging from 10 to 30 m. The upper layer consists of expansive clay and silty clay, while the lower layer consists of sandy loam and gravel. Since 1968, under the influence of reservoir water erosion and scouring, two sections have experienced collapses, separated by a distance of 60 m. The upstream collapsed section is approximately 120 m long, while the downstream collapsed section is about 600 m long, resulting in a bank retreat of over 35 m. (2) In the 1980s, the soil bank slopes near Liujiagou and Dashi Bridge in Xichuan County experienced minor collapses, but the extent was limited and had little impact. (3) Due to wave erosion effects, a wave-cut shelf has formed on the soft rock slopes of the red bed between Zhengjiagou and Houjiashan (west bank of the Liguangqiao Basin) in Xichuan County, with a maximum shelf height of approximately 2 m. The igneous rock, limestone, and slate slopes are primarily distributed around the Danhukou Danjiang Dam-Liguangqiao Basin, Xunlukou-Tongshugou, and Hankou Danjiang Dam-Xiaochuan, Xiejiaogou-Fenggou, and Huangjiaping-Gushan sections, exhibiting steep gradients, overall good stability, and only minor impacts from reservoir water, with essentially no evidence of bank collapse.

Bank failure is a phenomenon of slope deformation and instability that occurs after the dam is raised, resulting in elevated water levels, increased water depth, wider water surfaces, and intensified wave erosion, which disrupts the natural equilibrium of the original river valley banks. Under the influence of reservoir water, soil slopes composed of Quaternary loose sediment continuously collapse and retreat at a relatively slow rate, primarily characterized by horizontal displacement. In contrast, for rock slopes composed of soft rocks and loose gravel from the Cretaceous to the Lower Tertiary (K-E) red strata, river scouring undermines the toe of the slope, potentially creating undercuts or voids. The upper rock mass loses equilibrium and rapidly collapses or slides under the force of gravity, ultimately accumulating at the toe, with vertical displacement dominating the deformation process, resulting in sudden failures. 

For soil slopes, the main factors influencing bank failure are the morphology of the reservoir banks and the structure of the slopes. The higher and steeper the bank, the more severe the reconstruction; conversely, the lower and gentler the bank, the less severe. Steep underwater slopes with significant water depth at the toe experience strong wave and current action, leading to rapid transport of failure material and expedited reconstruction processes. Development of "chicken foot" shaped banks in tributary gullies leads to intense topographical cutting, with protruding points and convex banks surrounded by water, resulting in severe geomorphological changes, while concave banks experience relatively minor alterations. In wide river channels, concave banks facing flow experience strong wave erosion, resulting in significant reconstruction, whereas convex and rectilinear slopes experience relatively minor changes. 

For rock slopes, the main factors affecting bank failure include lithology, lithological composition, and rock mass structure. Furthermore, the development of structural surfaces within rock slopes can lead to unstable assemblages, with the fore slope's toe being overhanging and the slope in a state of limit equilibrium. Water storage in the reservoir can reduce the stability of the banks, potentially triggering bank failures or landslides. The bank failures and landslides that have occurred in the Danjiangkou Reservoir area exhibit characteristics of clustering, periodicity, and seasonality, closely linked to sustained heavy rainfall during flood periods.

Riverbank erosion and collapses are primarily distributed in the relatively broad and gentle valley sections of Huai Dong, Liu Shu Tang, and He Men Kou, often occurring in the Curved segments of the Quaternary concave banks where flow is diverted. Erosion effects are significant, frequently accompanied by bank collapses, which considerably impact slope stability and the distribution of landslides on the opposite bank. The assessment area indicates that only the riverbank erosion and collapse at the Xin Hong section in Huai Dong has a certain effect on the route. The proposed route from He Men Kou to Xia La Ba Gou section is located adjacent to the Wu Dong De Reservoir, where the embankment and slope may be subject to reservoir-induced bank collapses as a geological hazard. In the slope protection design, engineering and biological measures such as dry stone masonry revetments, rock throwing, retaining walls, and vegetation planting should be employed for the protection of embankments. Landslides with poor stability should be subjected to specialized engineering remediation measures. The protective measures against riverbank erosion and collapse geological hazards may include strengthening levees, rock throwing, and masonry revetments to prevent further development of riverbank erosion and collapses, thereby safeguarding the stability of the embankment.

Due to the influence of water flow, ice scour, wind-induced waves, and human activities, the Heilong River has experienced bank collapses, slumping, rolling, and channel diversion. The consequences are severe, posing risks to river stability, navigation channel alterations, vast land loss, cession of numerous islands to the Russian side, and threats to the lives and properties of local populations. According to existing data, the riverbanks from Xingdong to the mouth of the Songhua River collapse by 5 to 10 meters annually. Further downstream in the Khabarovsk section, the average bank retreat is between 8 and 20 meters per year over a length of 110 kilometers. There are five abandoned riverbanks that have been diverted, affecting areas such as Er Village's Tonglaba Island, the section from the head of the river to Jiejin Kou, the segment from E'tu to Le'deli, the stretch from Wangjiadian's Hou River to the mouth of the Zui River, and the section from Sancha Island to Fuyuan Town, encompassing a combined island area of approximately 100 square kilometers. The extent of land loss is alarming. Existing statistics indicate that there are over 100 areas of bank collapse along the Heilong River, totaling about 100 kilometers, with a collapse rate of 2 to 30 meters per year. Preliminary estimates reveal that due to the continuous lateral migration of the main navigation channel, approximately several tens of square kilometers of land resources are lost annually, resulting in significant collapses of arable land, buildings, streets, and other public infrastructure.

According to the direction of river erosion, it can be divided into downcutting and lateral erosion. The lateral erosion of rivers is the primary external force leading to bank collapse geological hazards. The cause of lateral erosion is mainly due to the transverse circulation generated at river bends, which flows down the concave bank, continuously eroding and abrading the bank. The lower part of the bank slope becomes hollowed out, causing the upper soil and rock mass to lose support and collapse under the influence of gravity. The concave bank is continually pushed laterally and downstream, forming high and steep cliffs or escarpments. The materials eroded from the concave bank are carried by the water flow and deposited on the convex bank, creating gentle floodplains that progressively widen. This increases the curvature of the riverbed, forming meanders, while also widening the valley floor, giving the cross-section of the valley a "U" shape. The lateral erosion of rivers that leads to bank collapse exhibits a strong periodicity (seasonality), predominantly occurring during the flood season when the water level rises to the fine sand, silt, and silty clay layers in the upper part of the riverbank (when the water level exceeds the critical erosion bank collapse threshold, equivalent to the p=50% flood level). Erosion-induced bank collapse can only occur under these conditions. The upper reaches of the Heilongjiang River are located in a mid-latitude region, primarily supplemented by rainfall, along with seasonal snowmelt and groundwater recharge. Rainfall accounts for about 75% to 80% of the total runoff. Since rainfall is concentrated in July and August, the river water level is high, flow velocity is fast, and discharge is large during this period, making it the primary time for erosion-induced bank collapse.

The geological hazard of bank collapse in Heilongjiang occurs under specific geomorphological, geological, climatic, and hydrological conditions. Its influencing factors are multifaceted, primarily categorized into natural and anthropogenic factors. These factors interact and exert influence on one another in a closely related manner. Among the natural factors, some take a long geological time to manifest, such as the effects of neotectonic movements. Others are relatively stable, including the structural integrity of rock and soil bodies, gravitational forces, and Coriolis effect. Some factors exhibit periodic changes, such as river erosion, intense rainfall scour, freeze-thaw cycles, wave erosion, and mechanical undermining by groundwater. Periodic factors are the primary contributors affecting bank collapse, with river erosion—especially during high water periods—being the most significant. The stable factors serve as the fundamental elements influencing bank stability, with the Coriolis effect and the structural integrity of riverside rock and soil bodies being the most essential. Anthropogenic factors tend to be localized and uncertain, serving as positive influencing factors that promote bank collapse.

The Ussuri River in Heilongjiang Province has a total length of 771 km. Due to the proximity of the Sikhote-Alin Mountains on the Russian side to the riverbank, while the majority of the riverbank on our side is situated in low-lying wetland, there are several sections along the bank prone to bank erosion, often located at concave shorelines. There are 28 locations of bank collapses along the Ussuri River, with a total length of 83 km. The rate of collapse generally averages 2 to 3 m·a^-1, with severe sections reaching 6 to 15 m·a^-1. For instance, in Raohe Town, Sipai Village, and Dong'an Town, an entire street was washed away over a span of 30 years from the 1940s to the 1970s. Raohe Island was originally part of the northern riverbank of Raohe Town but was cut off from the bank and became an island due to flooding in 1945, which caused a retreat of the riverbank by 25 m on our side. The former Soviet Union diverted the main shipping channel toward our side under the pretext of dredging the river, resulting in a bank collapse rate of 5 m·a^-1 on our side until we constructed revetments to halt the erosion. Additionally, New Kai Liu Island, Tuan Mountain Island, and Daxitong Island have also experienced shifts in the main navigation channel due to anthropogenic or natural sedimentation changes, which, following the international practice of designating the main navigation channel as a boundary, has naturally deprived our country of ownership over these islands. The river has developed an intricate network, with a gentle slope and low riverbanks, leading to significant instability of the river channel and banks during high water levels, causing flooding overflows and erosion. For example, in Wusu Town, Fuyuan County, due to aging revetment works from 1981 to 1985, the old revetment slope lost stability as revetment footings broke away and were washed away by floods, creating a gully 300 m long, averaging 9 m wide and 1 m deep. Similar issues of revetment aging leading to bank erosion exist in 10 locations within Fuyuan County. The bank morphology of the Ussuri River at Tonghua Village in Hulin City is of a first-class terrace, with the upper layer being clay and silt and the lower layer being gravel. Under the scouring effects of flowing water, collapses occur with a rate of 5 m per year. The main navigation channel of the Ussuri River was originally on the right side of New Kai Liu Island, Tuan Mountain Island, and Daxitong Island but has shifted to the left side of the islands due to bank collapses, resulting in a natural loss of our ownership of these islands. In summary, on the side of the Sino-Russian boundary river, there are a total of 247 locations of bank erosion, accumulating to 517.41 km in length. Among them, there are 19 extremely severe sections (collapse rate greater than 10 m·a^-1) totaling 64.6 km; 34 severe sections (collapse rate of 6 to 10 m·a^-1) totaling 87.47 km; 144 relatively severe sections (collapse rate of 1 to 5 m·a^-1) totaling 296.94 km; and 50 lighter sections (collapse rate less than 1 m·a^-1) totaling 68.4 km.

**Bank Collapse Morphology:**
Block collapses occur on the concave bank of a meandering river channel. Due to inertia, centrifugal force results as river water flows with faster velocity in the upper layer compared to the lower layer, thus the centrifugal force is maximal near the water surface and quickly diminishes downward. Under the influence of this centrifugal force, river water moves from the convex bank to the concave bank, causing an increase in water level on the concave side, leading to a transverse hydraulic gradient. This transverse gradient generates a result where river water flows towards the concave bank, creating a lateral flow that opposes the direction of the water flow at the bottom, i.e., surface water flows from the convex bank to the concave bank while at the riverbed it flows from the concave bank to the convex bank, thus forming a unidirectional circulation. This unidirectional circulation flows down the slope of the concave bank, continuously eroding and hollowing out the gravel at the base of the bank, which causes the upper silty clay to lose support and collapse. Consequently, the concave bank retreats both upstream and downstream, while the eroded material from the concave bank is transported by the bottom flow and deposited on the convex bank, leading to its continuous advancement. Therefore, the concave section of the riverbank is a frequent site for collapses. To control the collapse, certain concave sections utilize stone masonry revetments. As the top of the convex bank approaches the river edge, the original masonry revetment may lack sufficient depth, creating conditions conducive to large-scale block collapses. The transverse flow generates a diversion directed toward the slope at the toe of the masonry structure, causing erosion of the first layer of sand beneath it, leading to rapid sediment loss and development towards the top and inward of the slope, forming an increasingly larger erosion cavity. As the erosion cavity enlarges, the masonry structure and the topsoil layer act like an inverted bowl over the water surface, momentarily stabilizing the riverbank; however, when the erosion cavity continues to expand and the topsoil layer loses support, the riverbank collapses along the cavity and develops both laterally and inward, resulting in a significant portion of the riverbank being destroyed, forming a “block collapse.” Block collapses typically occur during the flood season at low tide. 

3.1.2 **Stripped Collapse:** In areas where transverse circulation is not well developed and river flow is generally parallel to the riverbank, the sand mass below the water line erodes due to "water-induced sand flow," leading to the loss of support in the surface soil layer and subsequent collapse. Its developmental process is gradual, characterized by simultaneous erosion and collapse, with the width of the collapsed area gradually increasing, not only washing away islands in the river but often damaging the riverbank. This type of collapse, which develops from a shallow to a strip form over a longer riverbank segment, is referred to as stripped collapse. Stripped collapses are nearly observable throughout the year.

3.1.3 **Pothole Collapse:** Primarily occurs on steep and elevated concave bank segments, which can be divided into two forms: one occurs during high water levels when river water reaches the sandy layer at the toe of the slope. The lateral flow action erodes this sandy layer, forming a void, while the overlying cohesive soil or sand layer collapses downward in a chicken-nest shape due to gravitational forces. The other form develops in similar flowing conditions, resulting in the formation of eroded deep troughs or scours along the bank. When these deep troughs or scours approach the riverbank, they cause unloading at the toe, leading to the formation of a pothole collapse.

Coastal Landslide Morphology: The sliding and dislocation landslides are exclusively developed in the Baidushan shoreline section of Jiayin County. This section is comprised of grayish-white sandstone from the Cretaceous Period's Furuo Formation, which exhibits weak cementation. The leading edge has experienced severe landsliding, with local areas exhibiting a shingle-like composite landslide. The new landslide mass bulges into the river and is quickly eroded away by the water. 

3.2.2 Gravitational Collapse Landslide The gravitational collapse landslides primarily occur in riverbanks with slopes greater than 45°, particularly in canyons, steep cliffs, and rock masses with steep inclinations, as well as in areas of new tectonic activity. The upstream section of the Heilongjiang River is situated in a cold climate zone, characterized by significant daily and seasonal temperature variations, leading to intense freeze-thaw weathering. Gravitational collapse landslides are well developed here, predominantly located in areas with stone seas and stone rivers, while they diminish in the midstream section. The development of fractures and joints in the riverbank rock mass or the long-term erosion of the toe soil by water and waves creates favorable conditions for gravitational collapse landslides.

To understand the geological hazard characteristics and distribution patterns of the Jin Chuan Hydropower Station project and its surrounding area in the upper reaches of the Dadu River, this study focuses on the main river and its tributaries within a 40 km radius of the dam area. Utilizing a combination of 1:10,000 high-precision remote sensing image interpretation, drone aerial photography, engineering geological surveying, and engineering geological profile measurements, this research validated the types and quantities of geological hazards and examined the relationships between geological hazards and factors such as topography, geological structure, engineering geological formations, slope structure, and hydrogeological conditions. 

The results indicated that a total of 38 geological hazards were identified within the study area, primarily consisting of debris flows, collapses, bank erosion, unstable slopes, and landslides, with debris flows and collapses being the most prevalent, accounting for 28.95% and 23.68% respectively. Collapses, landslides, unstable slopes, accumulations, and bank erosion are predominantly found in the Dadu River valley area at elevations below 2,600 m. The slope gradient for the distribution of landslides and unstable slopes primarily ranges from 25° to 45°, while the gradient for collapses generally exceeds 50°. The slopes of accumulations and bank erosion typically range from 20° to 35°. Geological hazards are concentrated in areas with an elevation difference of 200 to 500 m. The planar curvature shows that linear slopes account for 48.1% of the developed geological hazards, and the profile curvature reveals that linear slopes are involved in 59.3% of the geological hazards. The study area is divided into eight geological formations, with geological hazards frequently occurring within 100 m of the river. 

The findings of this research provide a more scientifically accurate basis for regional geological hazard prevention and control efforts.

Bank collapse refers to the deformation and destruction phenomena occurring on river and lake banks due to the erosive action of surface water flow and the undermining effects of groundwater. Common types include landslides, collapses, slumps, and debris flows. The formation of bank collapses is influenced by factors such as slope gradient, geological characteristics of the soil and rock mass, wave action, and human activities. Reservoir bank collapse is a typical manifestation; following water accumulation, the geological body of the bank undergoes collapse due to changes in hydrological conditions, leading to sedimentation of reservoir capacity, obstruction of water withdrawal structures, damage to adjacent farmland and infrastructure, and in severe cases, poses a threat to dam safety. Prevention and control measures include slope protection engineering, interception and drainage systems, and stone dumping for stabilization, while also necessitating the integration of bank collapse prediction models for risk assessment. Research indicates that the bank collapse process exhibits sudden characteristics, and human activities, such as riverbed sand extraction, exacerbate the risk of bank collapse. A notable case includes the collapse of agricultural land and damage to hydraulic structures along the Turbulent River due to sand mining. Current bank collapse prevention and control technologies have advanced to three-dimensional modeling prediction and parameter sensitivity analysis, providing quantitative foundations for engineering management.
